title: Shaker Peg Board (No Saw Cuts Required)
summary: A wall-mounted Shaker peg rail from one pre-cut board and a few turned pegs — no saw needed. Drill, finish, hang. The simplest useful woodworking project there is.
description:
This is the perfect first project or quick gift: a Shaker peg rail made from a single board that comes pre-cut to size, so there are literally no saw cuts required — you drill a few holes, glue in turned Shaker pegs, finish it, and hang it. Despite the simplicity it's genuinely handsome, and it teaches clean drilling and a good finish, which matter on every project after it.

The two decisions that lift it above a plain board: an eased edge and the finishing order. A simple chamfer or router profile on the front edges reads as "crafted" rather than "a board with pegs." And you must choose whether to finish then drill, or drill then finish — finishing first is easier, but drilling afterward can craze the finish around the holes, so drilling first and finishing after gives the cleaner result if you're patient.

Sand well through the grits (150, 180, 220 is ideal; 150 at a minimum with a sanding block) so the finish lays down smooth. Drill the peg holes to match your pegs' tenon diameter for a snug glue fit, space them evenly, glue the pegs in, and finish — a wipe-on poly or an oil-varnish like Arm-R-Seal looks great, and hardwoods like walnut or maple need no stain at all. Hang it into studs or with anchors sized to what you'll hang on it.

tools.note: Drill the peg holes to your pegs' tenon diameter.
Or a sanding block — step through 150/180/220 grit.
For a decorative edge; a simple chamfer by hand also works.

materials.name: Board, 24" x 3-1/2" x 1/2"
materials.note: Comes pre-cut to size — no saw needed. Hardwoods need no stain.

materials.name: Keyhole hangers or screws
materials.note: Size to what you'll hang on it; into studs or anchors.

cutList.note: Bought pre-sized — no cutting required. Ease the front edge with a chamfer or router.

steps.title: Cut all the parts
steps.body: Cut one peg rail board at 24" from the Hardwood or pine. Square every cut and label each piece as it comes off the saw.

steps.title: Ease the edge and sand
steps.body: Decide on an edge treatment — a router profile or a simple hand chamfer gives it a crafted look. Then sand the faces well, stepping through 150, 180, and 220 grit for the best result (150 minimum with a sanding block).

steps.title: Decide finish-then-drill or drill-then-finish
steps.body: Choose your order: finishing first is easier, but drilling afterward can craze or crack the finish around the holes. Drilling first and finishing after gives the cleanest holes if you're willing to take the extra care.

steps.title: Drill and glue the pegs
steps.body: Mark the peg positions evenly, drill holes to match your pegs' tenon diameter for a snug fit, add a little glue in each hole, and seat the pegs. Wipe any squeeze-out before it sets.

steps.title: Finish and hang
steps.body: Apply a wipe-on poly or an oil-varnish like Arm-R-Seal — hardwoods like walnut look great with no stain. When cured, hang the rail with keyhole hangers or screws into studs, or anchors sized to the load you'll put on the pegs.

images.alt: Shaker peg rail with turned pegs mounted on a wall
